headline: MACC recommended charges over the RM1.1B ARM chip deal. The deal continues.
context: On 6 May 2026, MACC said it would recommend charges against two over Malaysia's RM1.1 billion deal with British chip-design firm ARM Holdings, signed by MIDA in March 2025. The Economy Ministry says the deal continues. Former minister Rafizi added 28 pages of evidence on 14 May.

cards.big: Rafizi handed MACC 28 pages and a USB drive on 14 May, eight days after the agency recommended charges over the RM1.1 billion ARM Holdings chip deal.
cards.sub: MIDA signed with ARM on 5 March 2025 while he was Economy Minister. MACC has not named the two it recommends for charges.

cards.big: Akmal Nasrullah, the new Economy Minister, said on 23 February the deal continues. On 11 May he handed ARM chip-design tokens to SkyeChip, Oppstar and GreatAsic.
cards.sub: Cabinet's stated rationale: shift Malaysia's chip industry from back-end assembly to front-end design. The criminal probe runs in parallel.

cards.big: Azam Baki announced the charging recommendation on 6 May 2026. His tenure as MACC chief ended six days later, on 12 May.
cards.sub: The offences cited: abuse of power (MACC Act s.23), cheating (Penal Code s.420), criminal breach of trust (s.409). The Attorney-General's Chambers decides whether to charge.

cards.big: Rafizi resigned in May 2025 after losing the PKR deputy-president race to Nurul Izzah Anwar, the prime minister's daughter, by more than two to one.
cards.sub: He has been openly critical of the Madani government since. Nik Nazmi, who resigned the same week, called the probe a 'political machination.'

cards.big: MACC recommended charges against the signers. The same government defends the deal. The rule a billion-ringgit IP licence should have cleared is still not public.
The deal continues. MACC recommended charges. The AGC will decide. The same coalition has been in office for all three.
